excel.name: Excel
excel.overview:
📚 Your Excel Learning Path:

1️⃣ Week 1: Formulas (SUM, AVERAGE, IF)
2️⃣ Week 2: VLOOKUP & HLOOKUP
3️⃣ Week 3: Pivot Tables
4️⃣ Week 4: Charts & Data Visualization

Type "week 1" to start, or "next" anytime to continue!

weeks.title: Week 1: Excel Formulas
weeks.lesson:
📖 LESSON: Basic Formulas

Every Excel formula starts with = sign.

🔹 SUM: Adds numbers in a range
   =SUM(A1:A10) → adds all values from A1 to A10

🔹 AVERAGE: Finds the mean
   =AVERAGE(B1:B20) → average of 20 values

🔹 IF: Makes decisions
   =IF(A1>100, "Over budget", "OK")
   → checks if A1 is greater than 100

🔹 COUNT: Counts cells with numbers
   =COUNT(A1:A50) → how many cells have values

🔹 MAX / MIN: Find highest and lowest
   =MAX(A1:A10) → largest number in range
weeks.practice:
📝 PRACTICE TASKS:

1. Open a new spreadsheet. In column A, enter your expenses for 7 days.
2. In cell B1, use =SUM(A1:A7) to get total spending.
3. In cell B2, use =AVERAGE(A1:A7) for daily average.
4. In cell B3, use =IF(B1>5000, "Over budget!", "Within budget") to check.
5. In cell B4, use =MAX(A1:A7) to find your highest spending day.

Try it now and tell me your results!
weeks.quiz:
🧠 QUICK QUIZ:

1. What does =SUM(A1:A5) do?
   a) Multiplies values  b) Adds values  c) Counts values

2. =IF(10>5, "Yes", "No") returns what?
   a) "Yes"  b) "No"  c) Error

3. Which formula finds the lowest number?
   a) =MAX()  b) =LOW()  c) =MIN()

4. Every Excel formula must start with?
   a) +  b) =  c) #

✅ Answers: 1-b, 2-a, 3-c, 4-b

Type "next" for Week 2!

weeks.title: Week 2: VLOOKUP & HLOOKUP
weeks.lesson:
📖 LESSON: Lookup Functions

These find data in large tables — like searching a phonebook.

🔹 VLOOKUP (Vertical Lookup):
   =VLOOKUP(search_value, table_range, column_number, FALSE)

   Example: You have a product list:
   A1: Apple  B1: ₹100
   A2: Banana B2: ₹40
   A3: Milk   B3: ₹60

   =VLOOKUP("Milk", A1:B3, 2, FALSE) → returns ₹60

🔹 HLOOKUP (Horizontal Lookup):
   Same concept but searches in rows instead of columns.

🔹 INDEX + MATCH (Advanced alternative):
   =INDEX(B1:B3, MATCH("Milk", A1:A3, 0))
   More flexible than VLOOKUP — can look left!
weeks.practice:
📝 PRACTICE TASKS:

1. Create a table: Column A = Student names (5 students), Column B = Marks.
2. In cell D2, type a student name.
3. In cell E2, use =VLOOKUP(D2, A1:B5, 2, FALSE) to find their marks.
4. Try changing the name in D2 — the marks should update automatically!
5. Bonus: Add Column C = Grade. Now use column_number 3 to lookup grade.

This is exactly how companies look up employee data from databases!
weeks.quiz:
🧠 QUICK QUIZ:

1. VLOOKUP searches in which direction?
   a) Left to right  b) Top to bottom  c) Right to left

2. What does FALSE mean in VLOOKUP?
   a) Approximate match  b) Exact match  c) No match

3. If VLOOKUP can't find the value, it returns:
   a) 0  b) #N/A error  c) Blank

4. HLOOKUP searches in which direction?
   a) Vertically  b) Horizontally  c) Diagonally

✅ Answers: 1-b, 2-b, 3-b, 4-b

Type "next" for Week 3!

weeks.title: Week 3: Pivot Tables
weeks.lesson:
📖 LESSON: Pivot Tables — Excel's Most Powerful Feature

A Pivot Table summarizes thousands of rows into a clean report in seconds.

🔹 What it does:
   Raw data: 500 expense entries → Pivot Table: Total by category

🔹 How to create one:
   1. Select your data (including headers)
   2. Insert → Pivot Table → OK
   3. Drag fields:
      • Rows: Category (Food, Transport, etc.)
      • Values: Amount (it auto-sums!)

🔹 You can add:
   • Filters: Show only this month
   • Columns: Split by month
   • Multiple values: Sum AND Count

🔹 Why it's powerful:
   • No formulas needed
   • Updates with one click (Refresh)
   • Can create charts from pivot data
weeks.practice:
📝 PRACTICE TASKS:

1. Create a table with 20 rows: Date | Category | Amount
   Use categories: Food, Transport, Shopping, Bills
   Fill in sample data for 20 expenses.

2. Select all data → Insert → Pivot Table.

3. Drag "Category" to Rows, "Amount" to Values.
   → You now see total spending per category!

4. Drag "Date" to Filters. Now filter for specific dates.

5. Right-click a value → "Sort Largest to Smallest"
   → See which category costs you the most!

This is how businesses analyze millions of rows of data!
weeks.quiz:
🧠 QUICK QUIZ:

1. A Pivot Table is used to:
   a) Format cells  b) Summarize large data  c) Create formulas

2. To create a Pivot Table, go to:
   a) Home tab  b) Insert tab  c) Data tab

3. Dragging a field to "Values" typically does:
   a) Counts items  b) Sums numbers  c) Both (depends on data type)

4. To update a Pivot Table after changing source data:
   a) Delete and recreate  b) Click Refresh  c) It updates automatically

✅ Answers: 1-b, 2-b, 3-c, 4-b

Type "next" for Week 4!

weeks.title: Week 4: Charts & Data Visualization
weeks.lesson:
📖 LESSON: Charts & Visualization

Data tells a story — charts make that story visual.

🔹 Bar/Column Chart:
   Best for: Comparing categories (spending by category)
   Select data → Insert → Bar Chart

🔹 Line Chart:
   Best for: Trends over time (daily expenses this month)

🔹 Pie Chart:
   Best for: Showing proportions (% of budget per category)
   ⚠️ Use only when you have < 6 categories

🔹 Combo Chart:
   Mix bar + line to show two different metrics

🔹 Formatting Tips:
   • Always add a title
   • Label your axes
   • Use consistent colors
   • Remove chart clutter (gridlines, unnecessary legends)
   • Data labels > legend when possible
weeks.practice:
📝 FINAL PROJECT:

Create a "Personal Finance Dashboard" in Excel:

1. Sheet 1: Raw data — 30 days of expenses (Date, Category, Amount)
2. Sheet 2: Pivot Table summarizing by category
3. Sheet 3: Dashboard with:
   • Pie chart → spending % by category
   • Line chart → daily spending trend
   • Bar chart → category comparison
   • A cell showing =SUM for total spent
   • A cell showing =AVERAGE for daily average
   • An IF formula showing "Over budget" or "On track"

🎓 Congratulations! You've completed the Excel course!
You now know: Formulas, VLOOKUP, Pivot Tables, and Charts.

Type "certificate" to see your completion summary!
weeks.quiz:
🧠 FINAL QUIZ:

1. Best chart for comparing categories?
   a) Pie  b) Bar  c) Line

2. Best chart for showing trends over time?
   a) Pie  b) Bar  c) Line

3. Pie charts work best with:
   a) 2-5 categories  b) 10+ categories  c) Time series data

4. What should every chart have?
   a) Gridlines  b) A title  c) 3D effects

✅ Answers: 1-b, 2-c, 3-a, 4-b

🎓 Course complete! Type "certificate" for your summary!

python.name: Python
python.overview:
📚 Your Python Learning Path:

1️⃣ Week 1: Variables, strings & basic operations
2️⃣ Week 2: Lists, loops & conditions
3️⃣ Week 3: Functions & file handling
4️⃣ Week 4: Mini-project — Personal Expense Tracker!

Type "week 1" to start, or "next" anytime to continue!

weeks.title: Week 1: Python Basics
weeks.lesson:
📖 LESSON: Variables & Data Types

🔹 Variables — storing data:
   name = "Gayatri"        # String (text)
   age = 22                # Integer (whole number)
   height = 5.4            # Float (decimal)
   is_student = True       # Boolean (True/False)

🔹 Printing output:
   print("Hello, " + name)  → Hello, Gayatri
   print(f"Age: {age}")     → Age: 22

🔹 Taking input from user:
   name = input("Enter your name: ")
   age = int(input("Enter your age: "))

🔹 Basic math:
   total = 10 + 5    # 15
   avg = total / 2   # 7.5
   power = 2 ** 3    # 8 (2 raised to 3)
   remainder = 10 % 3 # 1
weeks.practice:
📝 PRACTICE TASKS:

1. Create a file called hello.py
2. Write:
   name = input("What is your name? ")
   age = int(input("How old are you? "))
   print(f"Hello {name}! You will be {age + 1} next year.")

3. Run it: python hello.py

4. Now try a calculator:
   a = float(input("Enter first number: "))
   b = float(input("Enter second number: "))
   print(f"Sum: {a+b}, Product: {a*b}, Average: {(a+b)/2}")

5. Bonus: Create a program that converts Celsius to Fahrenheit:
   F = (C × 9/5) + 32
weeks.quiz:
🧠 QUICK QUIZ:

1. What type is: name = "Hello" ?
   a) Integer  b) String  c) Boolean

2. What does print(10 % 3) output?
   a) 3  b) 1  c) 3.33

3. How do you take user input in Python?
   a) scan()  b) input()  c) read()

4. What is the output of: print(2 ** 4) ?
   a) 8  b) 16  c) 6

✅ Answers: 1-b, 2-b, 3-b, 4-b

Type "next" for Week 2!

weeks.title: Week 2: Lists, Loops & Conditions
weeks.lesson:
📖 LESSON: Making Decisions & Repeating Tasks

🔹 Lists — storing multiple items:
   fruits = ["apple", "banana", "mango"]
   fruits.append("grape")     # add item
   fruits.remove("banana")    # remove item
   print(fruits[0])           # "apple" (first item)
   print(len(fruits))         # count items

🔹 If/Else — decisions:
   marks = 85
   if marks >= 90:
       print("Grade A")
   elif marks >= 70:
       print("Grade B")
   else:
       print("Grade C")

🔹 For loop — repeat for each item:
   for fruit in fruits:
       print(f"I like {fruit}")

🔹 While loop — repeat until condition:
   count = 0
   while count < 5:
       print(count)
       count += 1
weeks.practice:
📝 PRACTICE TASKS:

1. Create a shopping list program:
   items = []
   while True:
       item = input("Add item (or 'done'): ")
       if item == "done":
           break
       items.append(item)
   print(f"Your list has {len(items)} items:")
   for i, item in enumerate(items, 1):
       print(f"  {i}. {item}")

2. Grade calculator:
   Take 5 subject marks as input, store in a list.
   Print average, highest, lowest, and pass/fail (avg >= 40).

3. Bonus: Number guessing game — computer picks random number, user guesses!
weeks.quiz:
🧠 QUICK QUIZ:

1. fruits = ["a","b","c"]. What is fruits[1]?
   a) "a"  b) "b"  c) "c"

2. How do you add "mango" to a list?
   a) list.add("mango")  b) list.append("mango")  c) list.push("mango")

3. "break" in a loop does what?
   a) Pauses  b) Exits the loop  c) Skips one iteration

4. for i in range(3) runs how many times?
   a) 2  b) 3  c) 4

✅ Answers: 1-b, 2-b, 3-b, 4-b

Type "next" for Week 3!

weeks.title: Week 3: Functions & File Handling
weeks.lesson:
📖 LESSON: Reusable Code & Working with Files

🔹 Functions — reusable blocks of code:
   def greet(name):
       return f"Hello, {name}!"

   message = greet("Gayatri")  # "Hello, Gayatri!"

   def calculate_bmi(weight, height):
       bmi = weight / (height ** 2)
       if bmi < 18.5: return "Underweight"
       elif bmi < 25: return "Normal"
       else: return "Overweight"

🔹 Reading files:
   with open("data.txt", "r") as file:
       content = file.read()
       print(content)

🔹 Writing files:
   with open("output.txt", "w") as file:
       file.write("Hello from Python!\n")

🔹 Working with CSV:
   import csv
   with open("expenses.csv", "r") as file:
       reader = csv.reader(file)
       for row in reader:
           print(row)
weeks.practice:
📝 PRACTICE TASKS:

1. Create functions:
   def add(a, b): return a + b
   def is_even(n): return n % 2 == 0
   def celsius_to_f(c): return (c * 9/5) + 32
   Test each function with different inputs.

2. File-based diary:
   Write a program that:
   - Asks user for a diary entry
   - Saves it to diary.txt with today's date
   - Can read back all past entries

3. Bonus: Expense tracker that saves to CSV:
   - Add expenses (amount, category, date)
   - Save to expenses.csv
   - Read and show total spending
weeks.quiz:
🧠 QUICK QUIZ:

1. What keyword defines a function?
   a) function  b) def  c) func

2. What does "return" do?
   a) Prints output  b) Sends value back to caller  c) Stops the program

3. "with open()" automatically does what?
   a) Creates the file  b) Closes the file when done  c) Deletes the file

4. To write to a file, the mode is:
   a) "r"  b) "w"  c) "x"

✅ Answers: 1-b, 2-b, 3-b, 4-b

Type "next" for the Final Project!

weeks.title: Week 4: Mini-Project — Expense Tracker
weeks.lesson:
📖 FINAL PROJECT: Personal Expense Tracker

Build a command-line app that does what HER-OS Finance does!

🔹 Features to build:
   1. Add expense (amount, category, description)
   2. View all expenses
   3. Total spending
   4. Spending by category
   5. Save/load from file

🔹 Project structure:
   expenses = []  # list of dictionaries

   def add_expense():
       amount = float(input("Amount: "))
       category = input("Category: ")
       desc = input("Description: ")
       expenses.append({"amount": amount, "category": category, "desc": desc})

   def show_total():
       total = sum(e["amount"] for e in expenses)
       print(f"Total: ₹{total}")

   def by_category():
       cats = {}
       for e in expenses:
           cats[e["category"]] = cats.get(e["category"], 0) + e["amount"]
       for cat, total in cats.items():
           print(f"  {cat}: ₹{total}")
weeks.practice:
📝 BUILD IT:

1. Start with the code from the lesson above.

2. Add a menu loop:
   while True:
       print("\n1. Add expense")
       print("2. View all")
       print("3. Total")
       print("4. By category")
       print("5. Exit")
       choice = input("Choose: ")
       if choice == "1": add_expense()
       elif choice == "5": break

3. Add file saving — save expenses to expenses.json:
   import json
   with open("expenses.json", "w") as f:
       json.dump(expenses, f)

4. Add file loading at startup.

🎓 Congratulations! You've built a real Python application!
Type "certificate" for your completion summary!
weeks.quiz:
🧠 FINAL QUIZ:

1. expenses = [{"amount": 100}, {"amount": 200}]. What is:
   sum(e["amount"] for e in expenses)?
   a) 100  b) 200  c) 300

2. dict.get("key", 0) returns 0 when:
   a) Key exists  b) Key doesn't exist  c) Always

3. json.dump() does what?
   a) Reads JSON  b) Writes Python data as JSON  c) Deletes JSON

4. What module handles JSON in Python?
   a) csv  b) json  c) data

✅ Answers: 1-c, 2-b, 3-b, 4-b

🎓 Course complete! Type "certificate" for your summary!

communication.name: Communication Skills
communication.overview:
📚 Your Communication Learning Path:

1️⃣ Week 1: Active listening & observation
2️⃣ Week 2: Structuring your message (PREP method)
3️⃣ Week 3: Body language & vocal confidence
4️⃣ Week 4: Handling difficult conversations

Type "week 1" to start, or "next" anytime to continue!

weeks.title: Week 1: Active Listening
weeks.lesson:
📖 LESSON: The Power of Active Listening

80% of communication is listening, not speaking.

🔹 The 3 Levels of Listening:
   Level 1: Internal — you're thinking about what YOU'll say next (most people)
   Level 2: Focused — you hear their words and understand the meaning
   Level 3: Global — you notice tone, body language, what's NOT said

🔹 Active Listening Techniques:
   • Paraphrase: "So what you're saying is..."
   • Clarify: "Can you tell me more about...?"
   • Reflect: "It sounds like you feel..."
   • Summarize: "Let me make sure I understand..."

🔹 The 2-Second Rule:
   After someone finishes speaking, wait 2 seconds before responding.
   This shows respect and gives you time to think.

🔹 Avoid these listening killers:
   ✗ Checking your phone
   ✗ Interrupting
   ✗ Planning your response while they're talking
   ✗ Saying "I know exactly what you mean" (invalidating)
weeks.practice:
📝 PRACTICE TASKS:

1. In your next 3 conversations today, practice the 2-second rule.
   After they finish speaking, pause before you respond.

2. In one conversation, use paraphrasing:
   "So what you're saying is [repeat in your own words]?"
   Notice how the other person reacts.

3. For 10 minutes, have a conversation where you ONLY ask questions.
   No statements, no advice — just curious questions.

4. Journal: Write down one thing you learned about someone today
   that you wouldn't have noticed without active listening.

Reflect and tell me how it went!
weeks.quiz:
🧠 QUICK QUIZ:

1. What percentage of communication is listening?
   a) 20%  b) 50%  c) 80%

2. The 2-second rule means:
   a) Speak for only 2 seconds  b) Wait 2 seconds before responding  c) Pause every 2 seconds

3. "So what you're saying is..." is an example of:
   a) Interrupting  b) Paraphrasing  c) Summarizing

4. Level 3 listening includes noticing:
   a) Just words  b) Words + tone + body language  c) Only body language

✅ Answers: 1-c, 2-b, 3-b, 4-b

Type "next" for Week 2!

weeks.title: Week 2: PREP Method
weeks.lesson:
📖 LESSON: Structure Your Message with PREP

PREP makes any point clear and persuasive:

🔹 P — Point: State your main idea first.
   "I believe we should launch the feature next month."

🔹 R — Reason: Give the reason why.
   "Because our competitors are releasing similar features in Q2."

🔹 E — Example: Support with evidence.
   "For instance, Company X gained 30% users after their early launch."

🔹 P — Point: Restate your point.
   "That's why I recommend we move the launch to next month."

🔹 Why PREP works:
   • Keeps you focused (no rambling)
   • The audience hears your conclusion first
   • Evidence makes you credible
   • Repetition makes the point stick

🔹 Works everywhere:
   Meetings, emails, presentations, interviews, even casual conversations
weeks.practice:
📝 PRACTICE TASKS:

1. Write a PREP response for: "Should we work from home or office?"
   P: Your position
   R: Your reason
   E: A real example or data point
   P: Restate

2. Write a PREP for: "Why should you get this job?"
   This is essentially an elevator pitch.

3. In your next meeting or group discussion, consciously use PREP
   for one point you make. Notice if people respond differently.

4. Record yourself giving a 60-second PREP on any topic.
   Watch it back — did you stay structured?

Share your PREP responses with me!
weeks.quiz:
🧠 QUICK QUIZ:

1. What does PREP stand for?
   a) Plan, Review, Execute, Present
   b) Point, Reason, Example, Point
   c) Prepare, Rehearse, Express, Polish

2. In PREP, what comes FIRST?
   a) The example  b) The reason  c) Your main point

3. Why do we repeat the Point at the end?
   a) We forgot  b) Reinforcement  c) To fill time

4. PREP is useful for:
   a) Only presentations  b) Only emails  c) Any communication

✅ Answers: 1-b, 2-c, 3-b, 4-c

Type "next" for Week 3!

weeks.title: Week 3: Body Language & Confidence
weeks.lesson:
📖 LESSON: Non-Verbal Communication

55% of communication is body language, 38% tone, only 7% words.

🔹 Power Posture:
   • Stand tall, shoulders back, chin slightly up
   • Take up space (don't shrink)
   • 2-minute power pose before important meetings

🔹 Eye Contact:
   • Look at the triangle: left eye → right eye → forehead
   • Hold for 3-5 seconds, then briefly look away
   • In groups: share eye contact with everyone

🔹 Hand Gestures:
   ✓ Open palms = honesty and confidence
   ✓ Steepled fingers = authority
   ✗ Crossed arms = defensive
   ✗ Touching face = nervousness

🔹 Voice Confidence:
   • Speak 10% slower than you think you should
   • End statements with a downward tone (not up like a question)
   • Pause instead of using "um" and "uh"
   • Project from your diaphragm, not your throat
weeks.practice:
📝 PRACTICE TASKS:

1. Stand in a power pose for 2 minutes before your next meeting or call.
   Notice how it changes your confidence.

2. Record a 1-minute video of yourself explaining your project.
   Watch with sound off — what does your body language say?

3. In your next conversation, consciously:
   • Maintain eye contact (3-5 second triangle)
   • Keep your hands visible and open
   • Speak 10% slower

4. Replace filler words: Every time you want to say "um",
   just pause silently instead. Silence is powerful.

How did the power pose feel?
weeks.quiz:
🧠 QUICK QUIZ:

1. What percentage of communication is body language?
   a) 7%  b) 38%  c) 55%

2. A power pose should be held for:
   a) 10 seconds  b) 2 minutes  c) 10 minutes

3. Crossed arms typically signal:
   a) Confidence  b) Defensiveness  c) Interest

4. Ending statements with a rising tone sounds like:
   a) A command  b) A question  c) Confidence

✅ Answers: 1-c, 2-b, 3-b, 4-b

Type "next" for Week 4!

weeks.title: Week 4: Difficult Conversations
weeks.lesson:
📖 LESSON: Handling Tough Talks

🔹 The SBI Framework (Situation-Behavior-Impact):
   "In yesterday's meeting (Situation),
    when you interrupted me twice (Behavior),
    I felt my point wasn't valued (Impact)."

🔹 Starting difficult conversations:
   ✓ "I'd like to discuss something important. Is now a good time?"
   ✓ "I value our relationship, which is why I want to be honest."
   ✗ "We need to talk." (creates instant anxiety)
   ✗ "You always..." / "You never..." (triggers defensiveness)

🔹 Handling criticism:
   • Pause. Breathe. Don't react immediately.
   • Ask: "Can you give me a specific example?"
   • Acknowledge: "I can see how that was perceived."
   • Respond: "Here's what I'll do differently."

🔹 Saying no with grace:
   "I appreciate you thinking of me for this. Right now, I'm focused on [priority].
    I can help with this after [date]. Would that work?"
weeks.practice:
📝 FINAL PROJECT:

1. Write an SBI statement for a real situation:
   - A colleague who takes credit for your work
   - A friend who always cancels plans
   - A manager who gives vague feedback

2. Practice saying no:
   Write 3 "graceful no" responses for:
   - Extra work assignment
   - Weekend plans you don't want to attend
   - A request that crosses your boundary

3. Role-play: Find a friend, practice giving feedback using SBI.
   Then switch — practice receiving feedback gracefully.

🎓 Congratulations! You've completed the Communication course!
Type "certificate" for your summary!
weeks.quiz:
🧠 FINAL QUIZ:

1. SBI stands for:
   a) Speak, Believe, Inspire  b) Situation, Behavior, Impact  c) Start, Build, Improve

2. "You always do this" is bad because:
   a) It's too short  b) It triggers defensiveness  c) It's too polite

3. When receiving criticism, first you should:
   a) Defend yourself  b) Pause and breathe  c) Criticize back

4. A graceful "no" includes:
   a) Just "no"  b) An alternative or timeline  c) An excuse

✅ Answers: 1-b, 2-b, 3-b, 4-b

🎓 Course complete! Type "certificate" for your summary!

design.name: UI/UX Design
design.overview:
📚 Your Design Learning Path:

1️⃣ Week 1: Color theory & typography
2️⃣ Week 2: Layout principles & composition
3️⃣ Week 3: Tools (Canva/Figma basics)
4️⃣ Week 4: Create your personal brand kit

Type "week 1" to start, or "next" anytime to continue!

weeks.title: Week 1: Color & Typography
weeks.lesson:
📖 LESSON: Visual Foundations

🔹 Color Theory:
   • Primary: Red, Blue, Yellow
   • Complementary: Opposite on color wheel (Blue + Orange)
   • Analogous: Side by side (Blue + Green + Teal)
   • 60-30-10 Rule: 60% dominant, 30% secondary, 10% accent

🔹 Color Psychology:
   • Blue = Trust, calm (banks, tech)
   • Red = Energy, urgency (sales, food)
   • Green = Growth, health (eco, wellness)
   • Purple = Luxury, creativity (beauty, premium)
   • Your app uses: Plum + Rose + Gold = Premium feminine energy

🔹 Typography:
   • Serif (Times) = Traditional, trustworthy
   • Sans-serif (Arial) = Modern, clean
   • Max 2 fonts per design: 1 heading + 1 body
   • Hierarchy: Title (32px) > Subtitle (24px) > Body (16px) > Caption (12px)
weeks.practice:
📝 PRACTICE:

1. Go to coolors.co — generate 5 color palettes. Pick your favorite.
2. Go to fonts.google.com — pair a heading font with a body font.
3. Design a simple card (on paper or Canva) using:
   - Your color palette (60-30-10 rule)
   - Your font pair
   - A title, subtitle, and short paragraph
4. Share it and tell me why you chose those colors and fonts!
weeks.quiz:
🧠 QUIZ:

1. The 60-30-10 rule refers to:
   a) Font sizes  b) Color proportions  c) Layout grid

2. Blue is associated with:
   a) Urgency  b) Trust  c) Creativity

3. How many fonts should you use in one design?
   a) 1  b) Max 2  c) As many as needed

✅ Answers: 1-b, 2-b, 3-b

Type "next" for Week 2!

weeks.title: Week 2: Layout & Composition
weeks.lesson:
📖 LESSON: Arranging Elements

🔹 Whitespace: Empty space isn't wasted — it helps readability.
   More whitespace = more premium, professional feel.

🔹 Alignment: Everything should align to an invisible grid.
   Misaligned elements look amateur instantly.

🔹 Visual Hierarchy: Guide the eye with size and weight.
   Biggest, boldest thing = what you see first.

🔹 The Z-Pattern: Eyes scan in a Z shape on web pages.
   Put logo top-left, CTA top-right, content center, action bottom-right.

🔹 The F-Pattern: For text-heavy pages, eyes scan in F shape.
   Put key info in the first line and left side.

🔹 Contrast: Light on dark, big next to small.
   Without contrast, everything blends together.
weeks.practice:
📝 PRACTICE:

1. Take a screenshot of any website you like.
   Draw the Z or F pattern over it — where do your eyes go?
2. Design a simple landing page layout (pen & paper):
   - Logo, headline, subtext, CTA button, image
   - Apply alignment, hierarchy, and whitespace
3. Redesign a bad design: Find any ugly poster or flyer.
   Recreate it with proper alignment and hierarchy.
weeks.quiz:
🧠 QUIZ:

1. Whitespace is:
   a) Wasted space  b) Intentional breathing room  c) A design error

2. The Z-pattern applies to:
   a) Text-heavy pages  b) Simple landing pages  c) Mobile only

3. Visual hierarchy is created by:
   a) Color only  b) Size, weight, and contrast  c) Fonts only

✅ Answers: 1-b, 2-b, 3-b

Type "next" for Week 3!

weeks.title: Week 3: Design Tools
weeks.lesson:
📖 LESSON: Canva & Figma Basics

🔹 Canva (canva.com) — great for beginners:
   • Templates for social media, presentations, posters
   • Drag-and-drop, no design skills needed
   • Free tier is very capable
   • Best for: Quick social posts, presentations

🔹 Figma (figma.com) — professional tool:
   • Used by real UI/UX designers
   • Design app screens, websites, prototypes
   • Free for up to 3 projects
   • Best for: App design, collaborative work

🔹 Key Figma concepts:
   • Frames: Containers for your design (like artboards)
   • Auto Layout: Responsive spacing (like CSS flexbox)
   • Components: Reusable design elements
   • Prototyping: Link screens to simulate a real app
weeks.practice:
📝 PRACTICE:

1. Canva: Create a LinkedIn banner for yourself.
   - Use your color palette from Week 1
   - Include your name, role, and 3 skills

2. Figma: Create a simple mobile app screen:
   - Frame: 390 × 844 (iPhone size)
   - Add a header, 3 cards, and a bottom nav
   - Use auto layout for consistent spacing

3. Bonus: Recreate the HER-OS dashboard in Figma!
weeks.quiz:
🧠 QUIZ:

1. Canva is best for:
   a) Complex app design  b) Quick social posts  c) Coding

2. Figma's "Auto Layout" is similar to:
   a) CSS Grid  b) CSS Flexbox  c) CSS Float

3. Components in Figma are:
   a) Colors  b) Reusable elements  c) Animations

✅ Answers: 1-b, 2-b, 3-b

Type "next" for the Final Project!

weeks.title: Week 4: Personal Brand Kit
weeks.lesson:
📖 FINAL PROJECT: Build Your Brand Kit

Every professional needs a consistent visual identity.

🔹 Your Brand Kit includes:
   1. Logo or monogram (your initials, styled)
   2. Color palette (3-5 colors with hex codes)
   3. Font pair (heading + body)
   4. Social media templates (LinkedIn, Instagram)
   5. Email signature design
   6. Presentation template (3-5 slides)

🔹 Consistency rules:
   • Same colors everywhere
   • Same fonts everywhere
   • Same style of imagery (photos vs illustrations)
   • Same tone of voice in text
weeks.practice:
📝 BUILD YOUR BRAND KIT:

1. Choose your palette: 1 primary, 1 secondary, 1 accent + black + white
2. Choose your fonts: 1 display font + 1 body font from Google Fonts
3. Create in Canva:
   • LinkedIn banner
   • Instagram post template
   • Email signature
   • 1 presentation slide
4. Save all assets in a folder called "My Brand Kit"

🎓 Congratulations! You now have a professional visual identity!
Type "certificate" for your completion summary!
weeks.quiz:
🧠 FINAL QUIZ:

1. A brand kit ensures:
   a) Everything looks different  b) Visual consistency  c) More colors

2. How many fonts should a brand kit have?
   a) 1  b) 2 (heading + body)  c) 5+

3. Brand consistency means:
   a) Same colors, fonts, style everywhere  b) Changing style often  c) Using all Google Fonts

✅ Answers: 1-b, 2-b, 3-a

🎓 Course complete! Type "certificate" for your summary!
